coredata.dc:title: One-pot synthesis of fluorescent aminoclay and the ratiometric fluorescence detection of sunset yellow
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Yongming
Shi, Junyan
Wei, Chengwei
Fang, Tiancheng
Tao, Tao
coredata.dc:description: Herein, a facile one-pot solvothermal strategy was utilized for fabrication of fluorescent aminoclay (F-AC) using MgCl2, 3-aminopropyltrimethoxysilane and m-phenylenediamine as precursors. This approach provides a simple approach for scale-up synthesis of F-AC. The as-prepared F-AC exhibited blue-green fluorescence and good photostability. And pH significantly affects the fluorescence emission behavior of F-AC, and F-AC showed the strongest fluorescence at pH = 4.0. The fabricated F-AC was utilized for ratiometric fluorescence determination of sunset yellow with good sensitivity by virtue of the ratiometric fluorescence varies at 535 nm and 490 nm, and a linear relationship in the range of 0–30 μM was obtained with a LOD of 0.195 μM. Furthermore, the F-AC was utilized for anti-counterfeiting and fabricated as fluorescent gel and membrane, revealing the promising optical applications of F-AC.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescent aminoclay
One-pot synthesis
Ratiometric fluorescence
Detection
Sunset yellow

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Calculation of fluorescence quantum yield of F-AC
Fluorescence detection of sunset yellow using F-AC
Detection of sunset yellow in real samples with F-AC
Applications of F-AC in anti-counterfeiting, fluorescent hydrogels and membrane
pH effect on the fluorescence intensity of F-AC
Ratiometric fluorescence detection of SY with F-AC
Selectivity of F-AC for fluorescence detection of SY
Possible mechanism of F-AC for fluorescence detection of SY
Practical applications of F-AC for fluorescence detection of SY
Applications of F-AC in anti-counterfeiting, fluorescent hydrogels and membrane

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the synthesis of F-AC and the applications of the ratiometric fluorescence assay of sunset yellow and anti-counterfeiting and fabrication of fluorescent hydrogels and membrane.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of F-AC from APTMS at different excitation wavelengths. (B) Fluorescence emission spectra of F-AC from AEAPTMS at different excitation wavelengths. (C) Fluorescence emission spectra of F-AC from AEAEAPTMS at different excitation wavelengths. (D) Fluorescence excitation spectra of F-AC from APTMS, AEAPTMS and AEAEAPTMS.
(A) XPS survey spectrum of F-AC. (B) FT-IR spectrum of F-AC.
(A) The pH effect on the fluorescence intensity of F-AC in pH 2.0–10.0 (λex = 390 nm). (B) Fluorescence excitation spectrum of F-AC (50 mM pH = 4.0 HAC-NaAC buffer, λem = 490 nm).

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of F-AC in the presence of different concentrations of SY (50 mM pH = 4.0 HAC-NaAC buffer, λex = 390 nm). (B) The curve of the relationship between I535/I490 and SY concentration (inset: the linear section of the curve of the relationship between I535/I490 and SY concentration, (50 mM pH = 4.0 HAC-NaAC buffer, λex = 390 nm)).

(A) The selectivity analysis of F-AC for fluorescence determination of SY, light grey represents the effect of interference substances; dark grey is the effect of interference substances and SY; the concentrations of K3Fe(CN)6, tartrazine, congo red and SY are all 50 μΜ, the concentrations of other interference substances are all 100 μM, 1–10: blank, Ca2+, Cd2+, Cr3+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Na+, Ni2+; 11–20: Pb2+, Zn2+, Cl−, Br−, I−, citrate, cysteine, glutathione, arginine, glycine; 21–25: glucose, K3Fe(CN)6, tartrazine, congo red, SY (Conditions: 50 mM pH = 4.0 HAC-NaAC buffer, λex = 390 nm). (B) Normalized fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of F-AC and UV–vis absorption spectrum of 20 μM SY (Conditions: 50 mM pH = 4.0 HAC-NaAC buffer, λex = 390 nm, λem = 490 nm).

head.ce:title: One-pot synthesis of fluorescent aminoclay and the ratiometric fluorescence detection of sunset yellow

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Materials Science, Nanjing University of Information Science & Technology, Nanjing, 210044, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Materials Science
Nanjing University of Information Science & Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Materials Science, Nanjing University of Information Science & Technology, Nanjing, 210044, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, a facile one-pot solvothermal strategy was utilized for fabrication of fluorescent aminoclay (F-AC) using MgCl2, 3-aminopropyltrimethoxysilane and m-phenylenediamine as precursors. This approach provides a simple approach for scale-up synthesis of F-AC. The as-prepared F-AC exhibited blue-green fluorescence and good photostability. And pH significantly affects the fluorescence emission behavior of F-AC, and F-AC showed the strongest fluorescence at pH = 4.0. The fabricated F-AC was utilized for ratiometric fluorescence determination of sunset yellow with good sensitivity by virtue of the ratiometric fluorescence varies at 535 nm and 490 nm, and a linear relationship in the range of 0–30 μM was obtained with a LOD of 0.195 μM. Furthermore, the F-AC was utilized for anti-counterfeiting and fabricated as fluorescent gel and membrane, revealing the promising optical applications of F-AC.

A facile one-pot solvothermal strategy was proposed for synthesis of fluorescent aminoclay, which was utilized for ratiometric fluorescence determination of sunset yellow with good sensitivity and for anti-counterfeiting, and further fabricated as fluorescent gel and membrane, revealing the promising optical applications of F-AC.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Food colorants have been widely used in the food industry to improve the appearance, flavor, taste and color of foods make them more appealing and appetizing [1–4]. And most food colorants are chemically synthetic due to good stability, low cost and excellent color uniformity [5–7]. However, excessive use of synthetic food colorants is one of food security problems in many countries because of the harms to human health. Particularly for those azo colorants, these azo colorants can be reduced to potentially toxic and carcinogenic aromatic amines in human body and the environment [8]. Sunset yellow (SY), disodium salt of 2-hydroxy-1- (4-sulfonato phenyl azo naphthalene-6-sulfonate), is a yellow synthetic azo dye, which is commonly employed in foods, beverages, cosmetics and pharmaceuticals due to its bright color, low price and excellent stability [6,7]. But overconsumption of SY can lead to its accumulation in the body, and further hinders children's development and causes some health problems, such as hyperactivity disorder, mental retardation, impairment of brain, liver and kidney function, and suppression of immune system, etc [4,6,9]. The acceptable daily intake for sunset yellow was below 4.0 mg/kg bw/day according to the European Food Safety Authority guidelines [6,10]. And in China, the maximum allowed amount of SY used in general food is 0.1 g kg−1 (GB2760-2011) [2,7]. Consequently, it is of considerable significance for developing facile and sensitive approaches for rapid detection of SY.

To date, many techniques have been developed for determination of SY in different food samples [3], such as chromatography [11], capillary electrophoresis [12], electrochemical method [6,10,13], immunoassay [14], spectrophotometric method [9], fluorometric method [4,7,15] and so on. Though there are some merits in these traditional instrumental methods, such as excellent selectivity and high sensitivity, these methods still suffer from the use of expensive instruments, time-consuming, complicated operations and the demand for technical and professional personnel, making them not to be suitable for real-time monitoring food quality. Among these, fluorescent approaches have attracted great attentions on account of simplicity, cost-effectiveness and rapid response. With the rapid developments in nanotechnology, a variety of nanomaterials with outstanding features have been constructed lots of nanosensors [16–22]. Over the past years, some nanomaterials like silicon nanoparticles [23] and carbon quantum dots [2,7] have been demonstrated for fluorescent determination of SY with great improvement in detection performance. Yet, it is still necessary to develop new nanomaterials for fluorescence sensing SY.

Fluorescent aminoclay (F-AC) is novel fluorescent nanomaterials with excellent two-dimensional anisotropy. Importantly, F-AC can over the shortcomings of carbon quantum dots like purification and large-scale synthesis, and shows enhanced fluorescence and higher stability in solid. In the recent years, various strategies have been used for fabrication of F-AC. For instance, Li and co-workers have synthesized F-AC with yellow-green fluorescence by covalently grafting 6-(bis(pyridin-2-ylmethyl)amino)benzo[de]isochromene-1,3-dione to aminoclay [24]. And lanthanide complexes were also used to fabricate F-AC via a Michael-like addition reaction between a amino group of aminoclay and a Michael acceptor group [25]. Europium-based F-AC containing carbon quantum dots have been designed for ratiometric fluorescence sensing tetracycline. The blue emissive carbon quantum dots were first encapsulated in AC as the reference signal, and citric acid-Eu3+ was further grafted on the AC as the recognition unit [26]. These reported F-AC suffer from multi-step synthesis, the use of fluorescent organic molecules or expensive lanthanide, limiting their large-scale preparation and wide applications.

Herein, we have developed a facile one-pot approach for large-scale synthesis of F-AC with blue-green emission through solvothermal treatment of MgCl2, aminopropyltriethoxysilane and m-phenylenediamine in ethanol at 180 °C (Fig. 1 ). The F-AC was easily purified through simple centrifugation. The as-prepared F-AC exhibited excellent stability in solution and solid, and displayed the strongest fluorescence at pH 4.0. And the obtained F-AC was employed for rapid ratiometric fluorescence detection of SY in water sample and soft beverage with good selectivity and sensitivity. Furthermore, the F-AC was successfully employed for anti-counterfeiting and fabricated as fluorescent gel and membrane, exhibiting the promising optical applications of F-AC.

Magnesium chloride hexahydrate was obtained from Shanghai Macklin Biochenmical Co., Ltd. Ethanol and m-phenylenediamine purchased from Beijing J&K Scientific Co., Ltd. 3-Aminopropyltrimethoxysilane (APTMS) was bought from Beijing InnoChem Science & Technology Co., Ltd. 3-(2-Aminoethylamino)propyltrimethoxysilane (AEAPTMS) was provided by Adamas Reagent Ltd. 3-[2-(2-Aminoethylamino)ethylamino]propyl-trimethoxysilane (AEAEAPTMS) was offered by Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. Ultrapure water (>18.2 MΩ cm) was used throughout the experiment. Other reagents were of analytical grade and were used directly without further purification.

Fluorescence spectra were performed on a luminescence spectrometer (Shimadzu RF-6000, Japan). The ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were obtained on a UV–vis spectrometer (Shimadzu UV-3600 Plus, Japan) with a 1 cm path length cuvette. Fourier transformed infrared (FT-IR) absorption spectra were acquired on a Nicolet iS50 Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrometer (Thermo Scientific, USA) with the KBr pellet technique. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was measured on an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (K-Alpha, Thermo Scientific, USA). A powder diffractometer (Rigaku Co., Japan, Cu Kα 1.5406 Å, 40 kV, 40 mA, D/teX Ultra 250 detector) was used to collect the X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns. Fluorescence lifetime was collected from a FLS1000 spectrofluorimeter (Edinburgh, UK). FEI talos F200x G2 transmission electron microscope (TEM) was used to measure the morphology feature of F-AC. Photostability tests were tested at room temperature using the 365 nm light from a three-way ultraviolet analyzer (24 W, ZF-1, Hangzhou Qiwei Instrument Co., Ltd., Hangzhou, China). And photographs were obtained using smart phone.

In the procedure of synthesis of F-AC, 0.63 g of magnesium chloride hexahydrate was added into 15.0 mL ethanol. The mixture was stirred for 30 min. And 0.975 mL APTMS was dropped into the above mixture and was further stirred for 30 min. Then 15 mg m-phenylenediamine was added into the mixture. The obtained mixture was further stirred for 30 min, and was transferred into a 30 mL poly(tetrafluoroethylene)-lined autoclave, which was incubated at 180 °C for 6 h. After cooling to room temperature, the sample was centrifuged for 10 min at 12,000 rpm to remove the supernatant, and the precipitate was washed for several times with ethyl acetate. The light yellow product was stored at 4 °C for further use. The procedure of the F-AC prepared from AEAPTMS and AEAEAPTMS was similar to the above process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Calculation of fluorescence quantum yield of F-AC
ce:section.ce:para:
The relative fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of F-AC was measured using quinine sulfate as the reference. The quinine sulfate with QY of 54.0% was dissolved in 0.1 M H2SO4 [27]. And the QY of F-AC was calculated according to the following equation:
Q F-AC = Q s(A s/A F-AC)(I F-AC/I s)(n F-AC/n s)2
Where Q is the QY, A is the absorbance at 390 nm, I is the integral area of fluorescence emission spectrum at the excitation 390 nm, and n is the refractive index. In this study, n F-AC/n s = 1, and the absorbance of two solutions at 390 nm are below 0.1. The subscripts F-AC and s are F-AC and quinine sulfate, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of sunset yellow using F-AC
ce:section.ce:para: For the fluorescence detection of SY, the F-AC solution with the concentration of 2.7 mg/mL was prepared by dissolving the F-AC precipitate in water. Then, 0.75 mL pH = 4 HAc-NaAc buffer (0.2 M), 1.0 mL F-AC and different concentrations of SY were mixed together, and the mixture was diluted to be 3.0 mL with ultrapure water (CAUTION: SY is toxic to the environment. The waste should be recycled.). Then the above mixture was incubated at room temperature for 1 min. Finally, the fluorescence emission spectra of the mixture were recorded on the fluorescence spectrometer (λex = 390 nm).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of sunset yellow in real samples with F-AC
ce:section.ce:para: The standard addition method was utilized for determination of SY in tap water and soft beverage. Tap water was collected in the lab, and the soft beverage was bought from local supermarket. These real samples were first filtered by 0.1 μm filter membrane to remove the suspended substances. Afterwards, 60 μL water samples were added in the mixture of 750 μL 750 μL pH = 6 NaH2PO4–Na2HPO4 buffer, 1000 μL F-AC and different concentration of SY were mixed together, and water was added to keep the final volume to be 3.0 mL. After incubation for 1 min at room temperature, the fluorescence emission spectra were measured at the excitation wavelength of 390 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Applications of F-AC in anti-counterfeiting, fluorescent hydrogels and membrane
ce:section.ce:para: For the anti-counterfeiting application, cotton bud dipped in F-AC solution was used to write letters on weighing paper. After drying in the oven at 60 °C, photographs in daylight and 365 nm UV light was recorded with smart phone.

For the fabrication of fluorescent hydrogels, 50.0 mg sodium alginate was dissolved in 2.0 mL F-AC, and then 20 μL 0.1 M CaCl2 solution was dropped into the sodium alginate solution with vigorous shake. After shaking for about 2 min, the fluorescent hydrogels were successfully fabricated. And smart phone was used to record the pictures of fluorescent hydrogels in daylight and 365 nm UV light.

Furthermore, the fabrication of fluorescent membrane was conducted by casting and evaporation of the solvent. First, 0.2 mL glacial acetic acid was added into 10.0 mL F-AC, and 250 mg chitosan was dissolved in the mixture. Then, chitosan solution was poured into the clean Petri dish. The Petri dish was put in the oven at 60 °C for 12 h to remove the solvent, and the membrane was carefully removed from the Petri dish. Also, the pictures in daylight and 365 nm UV light was collected using smart phone.

The F-AC was fabricated through a one-pot solvothermal treatment of MgCl2, APTMS and m-phenylenediamine in ethanol at 180 °C. To obtain much brighter F-AC, three kinds of aminosilane (APTMS, AEAPTMS and AEAEAPTMS) were employed for preparation of F-AC. The resulted F-AC solutions exhibit light yellow, and the color of the F-AC solution from APTMS is darker than the other two solutions (Fig. S1A). And the blue-green fluorescence of the F-AC from APTMS under 356 ultraviolet (UV) light is much brighter than the other ones (Fig. S1C). After centrifugation, light yellow solids were collected. The amount of F-AC from APTMS is more than the other two F-AC (Fig. S1B). And strong fluorescence was observed in the three F-AC solids (Fig. S1D). Subsequently, the F-AC precipitates from APTMS, AEAPTMS and AEAEAPTMS were dissolved in water, respectively. Their fluorescence emission and excitation spectra were measured. The shape of fluorescence emission spectra of the three F-AC at different excitation wavelengths was similar with each other. But the maximum fluorescence emission wavelength was different. The maximum fluorescence excitation wavelength of F-AC from APTMS was at 340 nm. And both of the F-AC from AEAPTMS and AEAEAPTMS exhibit maximum fluorescence excitation wavelength at 350 nm. And their maximum fluorescence emission wavelengths are at 485 nm, 480 nm and 470 nm, respectively (Fig. 2 A, B and 2C). The shape of their fluorescence excitation spectra was analogous, and their maximum excitation wavelength was at 340 nm, 350 nm and 350 nm, respectively (Fig. 2D). Meanwhile, their UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded. The F-AC from APTMS and AEAEAPTMS showed maximum absorption wavelength at about 340 nm and 362 nm, respectively. And a shoulder peak at about 350 nm was observed in F-AC from AEAPTMS (Fig. S2). Further, I/A was used to compare the difference of their fluorescence, where I is the fluorescence intensity of F-AC at the maximum fluorescence emission wavelength, and A is the absorbance at the maximum fluorescence excitation wavelength, which is positively correlated with the concentration of F-AC. It was easily noticed that the F-AC from APTMS possessed the highest I/A (Fig. S3). Therefore, we chose the F-AC from APTMS for the following experiments.

Control experiments were used to elucidate the formation mechanism of F-AC. But the formation of F-AC was closely associated with the rich amino groups of aminoclay, as well as with the property of m-phenylenediamine. Aminoclay first formed in ethanol. The amino groups of aminoclay would react with m-phenylenediamine to form small carbon quantum dots via condensation reaction under solvothermal conditions. The small nanomaterials further grew into large nanomaterials in the layers of aminoclay as time goes on. The unique two-dimensional structure also restricted their interactions, generating distinct bright fluorescence behavior [28]. To verify the hypothesis, the solvothermal treatment of APTMS and m-phenylenediamine in ethanol was conducted under the same conditions. The obtained solution exhibited light yellow in visible light, which was similar with that of the F-AC. And weak blue fluorescence was observed in the solution. On the contrary, the F-AC showed bright blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV light, demonstrating that the unique of aminoclay contributed to the distinct fluorescence emission behavior of F-AC (Fig. S4). Moreover, the F-AC solid still showed bright fluorescence (Fig. S5). Furthermore, a two-step method for synthesis of F-AC was developed to study the effect of free APTMS on the formation of F-AC. The AC was first prepared under the same solvothermal conditions. And then the F-AC was prepared through solvothermal treatment of the resulted AC and m-phenylenediamine in ethanol under the same conditions. The resulted F-AC was not well dissolved in ethanol and but showed similar bright blue-green emission behavior under 365 nm UV light (Fig. S6). The experiment demonstrated that carbon quantum dots contributed to the fluorescence emission of F-AC, and the free APTMS was not necessary for the formation of F-AC.

The as-prepared F-AC displayed lamellar structure, and the shape was different with each other (Fig. S7), which may be attributed to the solvothermal effect. And XRD patterns were utilized to further elucidate the microstructure of F-AC. The non-sharp peaks indicated the semi-crystalline of F-AC [29]. The XRD pattern of F-AC exhibited four peaks with 2θ = 6.46° (d001), 23.1° (d002, 110), 36.58° (d130, 200), 58.98° (d060, 330), indicating that the F-AC possessed the similar talc-like structure of AC (Fig. S8). Based on the Bragg's law (λ = 2dsinθ), the interlayer spacing of F-AC was calculated to be 1.37 nm, which is larger than that of the AC [30]. The enlarged interlayer spacing of F-AC was attributed to the carbon quantum dots formed interlayers of AC. And the peak at 2θ = 58.98° corresponds to the intra-plane d060 smectite reflection, revealing the presence of the structural framework of aminoclay in F-AC [31].

The surface electronic states and chemical composition of F-AC were investigated using XPS. The XPS survey spectrum of the F-AC depicted the presence of C, N, O, Si and Mg (Fig. 3 A). The high-resolution XPS spectrum of C1s exhibited four peaks at 284 eV, 284.5 eV, 284.7 eV and 285.7 eV, 287.9 eV (Fig. S9A), which was corresponding to C–Si, CC, C–C, C–N and C–4N, respectively [32–34]. Two peaks at 397.7 eV and 399.1 eV were observed in the deconvoluted N1s spectrum (Fig. S9B), which could be ascribed to C–N and N–H/CN–C [34,35]. The O1s spectrum displayed three peaks at 531.2 eV, 531.7 eV, 532.7 eV and 534.1 eV (Fig. S9C), representing Mg–OH, Si–O–Mg, Si–O–Si and Si–O–H, respectively [36,37]. In the Si2p spectrum, the two peaks at 101.5 eV and 102.5 eV could be assigned to the Si–C and Si–O (Fig. S9D) [35,38]. And the peak at 1304 eV in Mg1s spectrum was attributed to Mg–O (Fig. S9E) [39]. The functional groups of F-AC were further measured by FT-IR (Fig. 3B). The broad peak at 3033 cm−1 was associated with N–H and O–H stretching vibrations [40]. And the symmetric stretching vibration of H–O–H bond from water adsorbed in F-AC might also belong to the peak [41]. Also, the peak might be related to the presence of sp2 C–H bonds [42]. The shoulder peak at 2936 cm−1 was indicative of the stretching vibrations of C–H, CH–H and O–H, and the deformation vibration for O–H [32,35]. The peak at 1595 cm−1 belonged to phenyl group and the N–H bending vibration [32,35,41]. The band at 1502 cm−1 was indicative of N–H bending vibration [43]. The peak at 1226 cm−1 was attributed to the C–O bending vibration [43]. The bands at 1022 cm−1 and 1150 cm−1 was assigned to the Si–O–Si stretching vibration [35,44,45]. The frequency at 938 cm−1 was due to the O–H inner-surface deformation vibration [45]. A peak related to the symmetric stretching vibration of Si–O–Si was observed at 790 cm−1 [37,41]. A band at 698 cm−1 was assigned to the Si–O symmetric stretching vibration [46]. The information from FT-IR spectrum was consistent with the XPS analysis results, demonstrating that there were rich amino groups on the surface of F-AC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: pH effect on the fluorescence intensity of F-AC
ce:section.ce:para: The pH effect on the fluorescence emission behavior of F-AC has been investigated. It was easily observed that pH exhibited significant effect on the fluorescence emission behavior of F-AC. With increasing pH value from 2.0 to 10.0, the maximum fluorescence excitation wavelength of F-AC shifted from 390 nm to 340 nm. Yet, the maximum fluorescence emission wavelength remained constant at 490 nm (Fig. S10), and the fluorescence intensity at 490 nm in pH = 2.0–4.0 showed the strongest, subsequently also gradually declined (Fig. 4 A). Thus, pH 4.0 was chose for the following experiments. This interesting phenomena may be attributed the rich amino groups on the surface of F-AC. These amino groups easily protonized in acidic conditions, stabilizing the F-AC and further enhancing the fluorescence intensity. Interestingly, the QY of F-AC in pH 4.0 was calculated to be approximately 9.85% by using quinine sulfate as the reference, which was enhanced to be three times as compared with that of the F-AC in water (Fig. S11).

Also, no obvious color was not observed in the diluted F-AC solution in pH = 4.0 under visible light (Fig. 4Ba). While bright blue-green fluorescence was found in UV light of 365 nm (Fig. 4Bb). And fluorescence excitation spectrum of F-AC in pH = 4.0 showed two peaks at 265 nm and 390 nm (Fig. 4B), which was consistent with its fluorescence emission spectrum. Subsequently, the photostability of F-AC was evaluated. After consecutively scanning for 40 times, the fluorescence intensity at 490 nm remained nearly constant (Fig. S12). And fluorescence intensity only reduced by 2.5% the after continuous 365 nm UV irradiation for 25 min (Fig. S13). Moreover, the F-AC solution stored in darkness and at room temperature for three months still remained strong fluorescence (Fig. S14). These results demonstrated the good photostability of F-AC, promising the wide applications of F-AC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric fluorescence detection of SY with F-AC
ce:section.ce:para: In light of the toxic impacts of SY on human health and the environment, the F-AC was utilized for fluorescence assay of SY. Firstly, the color and fluorescence change of F-AC solution in the absence and presence of SY were studied. The colorless F-AC solution easily changed to orange after the addition of 20 μM SY. Surprisingly, in 365 nm UV light, the fluorescence of the F-AC solution changed to yellow-green from blue-green, which was corresponding to the maximum fluorescence emission wavelength at 535 nm and 490 nm (Fig. S15). Subsequently, the incubation time of F-AC with SY on the ratio of fluorescence intensity at 535 nm and 490 nm (I535/I490) was investigated. Upon the addition of 20 μM SY, I535/I490 instantly reached the maximum, and remained unchanged within the subsequent 15 min, indicating the rapid response of F-AC to SY (Fig. S16).

Then different concentrations of SY were added into the F-AC solution to study the sensitivity. With increasing the concentration of SY, the fluorescence intensity at 490 nm continuously reduced. Meanwhile, a new peak at 535 nm gradually emerged (Fig. 5 A), which may be ascribed to the fluorescence emission of SY (Fig. S17). By virtue of the ratio of the fluorescence intensity at 535 nm and 490 nm (I535/I490), SY was successfully quantified with a linear range of 0–30 μM (Fig. 5B). The linear fitting equation was I535/I490 = 0.01767[SY]+0.46941 (R = 0.99). The limit of detection of was calculated to be about 0.195 μM based on the LOD formula (S/N = 3σ/k), where k is the slope of the calibration curve and σ is the standard deviation of blank samples. Additionally, the sensing performance of F-AC for fluorescence assay of SY was compared with those reported results. It was easily found that the performance of the F-AC for sensing SY was comparable to that of those reported sensing systems (Table S1), revealing the promising applications of the sensing system [2,4,23,47]. Notably, this proposed sensing system is more accurate that that of other reported methods because the ratiometric fluorescence response, which can avoid the disturbance from the fluctuation of the detection environment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of F-AC for fluorescence detection of SY
ce:section.ce:para: Selectivity is significant for the fabrication of sensors. To evaluate the selectivity of F-AC towards the fluorescence assay of SY, different metal ions, anions and organic substances at the concentration of 100 μM were added into the F-AC solution. It was easily found that these metal ions, anions and biomolecules showed no obvious effect on the I535/I490. Yet the presence of 50 μM SY except tartrazine could significantly enhanced the I535/I490 (Fig. 6 A). And when the colored substances such as tartrazine and congo red were mixed with SY, the I535/I490 decreased a bit, which was attributed the overlap between absorption spectra of these colored species and the fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of F-AC [48,49]. Yet, the presence of these colored species did not generate the fluorescence emission peak at 535 nm, which can be used to distinguish SY from colored species. But the mixture of SY and these colored species is difficult to distinguish SY (Fig. S18). Thus, the F-AC is suitable for detection of SY in aqueous solution without other colored species.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible mechanism of F-AC for fluorescence detection of SY
ce:section.ce:para: To better elucidate the possible detection mechanism of F-AC towards SY, the fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of F-AC were compared with the UV–vis absorption spectrum of SY. There was a peak at 481 nm and a shoulder peak at about 390 nm in the UV–vis absorption spectrum of SY, which partially overlapped with the fluorescence excitation spectrum of F-AC and significantly overlapped with the fluorescence emission spectrum of F-AC (Fig. 6B), indicating that inner filter effect (IFE) contributed to the fluorescence quenching at 490 nm. And fluorescence decay technique was further utilized to record the fluorescence lifetime of F-AC in the absence and presence of SY. It was easily noted that the presence of SY did not greatly affect the lifetime of F-AC (Fig. S19), further confirming that IFE facilitated the fluorescence quenching at 490 nm [50,51]. And for the new peak at 535 nm, the peak might arise from SY, SY could enter the interlayer of F-AC, enhancing the dispersibility of SY, further increasing the fluorescence of SY at 535 nm (Fig. S17). With the increase of SY concentration, the fluorescence intensity at 490 nm gradually reduced, and the new peak at 535 nm gradually increased. Based on the change of I535/I490, ratiometric fluorescence determination of SY was accomplished.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Practical applications of F-AC for fluorescence detection of SY
ce:section.ce:para: To confirm the feasibility of the proposed method, tap water from the lab and soft beverage from local supermarket were collected and measured by using our approach. The tap water and soft beverage were diluted to be 50 times and spiked with 6 μM and 20 μM SY. The samples were further measured with our method. Good recoveries were achieved, which demonstrated the practicability of the developed method for sensing SY in real samples (Table S2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Applications of F-AC in anti-counterfeiting, fluorescent hydrogels and membrane
ce:section.ce:para: In view of the strong and stable fluorescence, low-cost F-AC aqueous solution was employed as ink for writing letters on weighing paper. The letters were not easily identified in daylight, but were clearly displayed under 365 nm UV light (Figs. S20A and B). Importantly, the F-AC is low-cost, easily washable, invisible and stable, making the F-AC to be potentially applied in anti-counterfeiting. And the F-AC could be embedded in calcium alginate hydrogels to generate bright fluorescent hydrogels (Figs. S20C and D). Furthermore, F-AC was mixed with chitosan to fabricate blue-emitting fluorescent membrane (Figs. S20E and F). And the excellent transparency and flexibility further make the F-AC to be widely used in anti-counterfeiting. Compared with other fluorescent dyes-based fluorescent ink, hydrogels and membranes, our ones did not require complicated synthetic procedures and costly dyes. Meanwhile, these results demonstrated the excellent stability and potential optical applications of F-AC.

In conclusion, fluorescent aminoclay (F-AC) has been prepared via a one-pot solvothermal method by using MgCl2, 3-aminopropyltrimethoxysilane and m-phenylenediamine as precursors. This approach was economical, labor-saving and environmentally friendly. And this method provides a simple approach for scale-up synthesis of F-AC. The as-prepared F-AC exhibited blue-green fluorescence and good photostability. And pH greatly affects the fluorescence emission behavior of F-AC, which exhibited the strongest fluorescence at pH = 4.0. The obtained F-AC was utilized for ratiometric fluorescence detection of sunset yellow with good sensitivity based on the ratiometric fluorescence varies at 535 nm and 490 nm, and a linear scope ranging from 0 to 30 μM was achieved with a LOD of 0.195 μM. Moreover, the F-AC was utilized for anti-counterfeiting and fabricated as fluorescent gel and membrane, showing the promising optical applications of F-AC.

Yongming Guo: Conceptualization, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Junyan Shi: Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Chengwei Wei: Investigation. Tiancheng Fang: Investigation. Tao Tao: Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was financially supported by the Startup Foundation for Introducing Talent of NUIST, China (2018r056), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province, China (No. BK20201389), Qing Lan Project of the Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions of China (No. R2018Q03).
